posts.text: End-of-week reflection: In any job, some work is more fun than the rest. You find this work joyful in nearly any context. Even when it is hard, the time flies by because you find it fun, fulfilling, and personally satisfying. Everyone has this joy mode. We like our jobs more or less based on how much of our time we spend in our personal joy mode. I can enjoy many kinds of work. But for me, joy mode is clearly teaching, writing, and storytelling work. The more time I get to spend on this, the happier I am. What kind of work is your joy mode? ** I'm Kieran Snyder, an AI exec, former CEO, and data storyteller who publishes new data and comics about AI, management, and work at nerd processor. Give me a follow if you like this stuff!
posts.hook: End-of-week reflection: In any job, some work is more fun than the rest.
posts.name: Kieran Snyder

posts.text: Most SaaS companies are trying to AI-ify their offerings to stay relevant. As a rule, this looks like adding "do this task with AI" buttons and/or adding "AI teammates" into existing workflows. If I were leading a legacy SaaS company right now, this is not what I would do. I think there are only two plays for legacy SaaS companies: be the authoritative data source, or promise an outcome. BE THE AUTHORITATIVE DATA SOURCE If you're fortunate enough to be a system of record rather than a point solution, your customers have created mountains of proprietary business data from using your product. That data is locked up in your system. Even if your workflows are legacy, the data likely is still essential. Clean, real, authoritative data is gold for a company that is modernizing with AI; maybe workflows were your old value prop, but now it's the data and its usability. PROMISE AN OUTCOME In a world where workflows are getting commoditized, outcomes are highly differentiating. It's no longer enough to make it possible for customers to do work; they want the work itself to be completed. Most legacy SaaS companies are not comfortable promising outcomes. I get it! We promised outcomes early at Textio and got all the benefits and hellish scar tissue that stemmed from that. But in the current market, your customers are generally more interested in paying for an end result than for a bunch of workflows that need to be manually applied. Try as I might, I can't see other moves for legacy SaaS companies. What else am I missing? ** I'm Kieran Snyder, an AI exec, former CEO, and data storyteller who publishes new data and comics about AI, management, and work at nerd processor. Give me a follow if you like this stuff!
posts.hook: Most SaaS companies are trying to AI-ify their offerings to stay relevant.
posts.name: Kieran Snyder

posts.text: This moment in uninspired parenting happened in my house last week: Me, to 16yo old: It's just you and me for dinner tonight. What should we have? 16yo: I'm craving sushi. Me: Actually, I'm going to order pizza. See, by the time I asked the 16yo what she wanted for dinner, I wasn't really looking for input. I had already kind of decided to order pizza. There is nothing more frustrating to someone than when you ask for their feedback, but have little intention of using it. Most leaders have done this. Nearly every employee survey does this. This week’s comic shows this in action, and in the chat, I share more about why we do this even when we know better. In the end, my 16yo called me out on my fake feedback-asking. We ordered sushi. ❤️ ** I'm Kieran Snyder, an AI exec, former CEO, and data storyteller who publishes new data and comics about AI, management, and work at nerd processor. Give me a follow if you like this stuff!
posts.hook: This moment in uninspired parenting happened in my house last week: Me, to 16yo old: It's just you and me for dinner tonight.
posts.name: Kieran Snyder

posts.text: Last night, our 17yo had her boyfriend over for dinner for the first time. Our other two kids were out, so it was just parents + 17yo + boyfriend. Unfortunately for the boyfriend, dinner timing was midstream on a project they were doing where our daughter was dying his hair a wacky color. So he had to come down to dinner with his girlfriend's parents for the first time with his hair pulled into 20 neat little sections covered in bleach. 🤣 It takes a certain kind of confidence to pull that off and still show up ready to talk about your chemistry class and fencing lessons and summer plans at the dinner table. Going to think about this the next time I feel nervous about a work event where I have to be "on" but don't feel like it. In your next big meeting, may you have the confidence of a 17yo having dinner with his girlfriend's parents for the first time!
posts.hook: Last night, our 17yo had her boyfriend over for dinner for the first time.
posts.name: Kieran Snyder

posts.text: End-of-week reflection: No one is more fun to work with than an energized builder. This is someone who: * Is more focused on what might be possible than on what is impossible * Spends more time building than criticizing others * Offers feedback proactively and constructively to make things better * Makes work more fun You don't have to have a big personality. You don't have to exude toxic positivity. You do have to want the best, work for the best, and be willing to take risks and learn. I spent this week with my team in Mountain View and had great collaboration with many energized builders, so this is on my mind ending the week. Happy Friday! ☀️
posts.hook: End-of-week reflection: No one is more fun to work with than an energized builder.
posts.name: Kieran Snyder

posts.text: If you are not reading Nick Mehta's newsletter, you are missing out on the most human, moving writing from a tech leader that I've yet found on the internet. Consider this a (strictly unbiased) PSA. I have no stake in the newsletter. But every time I read an issue, I feel moved. And having just read this week's letter, I feel compelled to share it with others. That's what I have for you this Thursday. Happy reading!
posts.hook: If you are not reading Nick Mehta's newsletter, you are missing out on the most human, moving writing from a tech leader that I've yet found…
posts.name: Kieran Snyder

posts.text: This week's comic is inspired by something Daniel Chait tweeted many years ago, “Everyone else’s business looks easy from the outside.” It is a simple and elegant truth: everyone else's business does look easy from the outside, especially if it is successful. But I've worked with enough founders and executives to know that the appearance of ease is always, 100% of the time, a lie. People who are successful have usually chosen to optimize for something. But in choosing something to optimize for, you inherently trade off something else. E.g. * Want career stability? Work at one company for 25 years and become incapable of getting hired anywhere else. * Want autonomy? Drop your W-2 lifestyle and become permanently anxious about money. * Want relevance in AI? Build your fleet of superagents and become incapable of unplugging. * Want to be “mission driven”? Merge your entire personality with your employer and experience every reorg as a breakup. * Want to be indispensable? Become the only person who knows how anything works and spend every vacation secretly checking Slack from the bathroom. In the end, choices are just about tradeoffs. Optimizing in one area creates dysfunction in others. And optimizing to not make choices at all just creates another kind of dysfunction. Maturity is less about solving dysfunction than intentionally choosing which dysfunctions you can live with. Goes double for mature leadership. ** I'm Kieran Snyder, an AI exec, former CEO, and data storyteller who publishes new data and comics about AI, management, and work at nerd processor. Give me a follow if you like this stuff!
posts.hook: This week's comic is inspired by something Daniel Chait tweeted many years ago, “Everyone else’s business looks easy from the outside.” It i…
posts.name: Kieran Snyder

posts.text: Something that surprises many of my male colleagues: Most women who achieve professional success in public get hate mail. Often it is anonymous, though not always. It isn't always explicitly threatening, though sometimes it is. It doesn't happen every day, but even a few times a year is enough to create baseline expectations that are dysfunctional. If a man gets famous enough, this happens to him too. But the fame bar for women to attract trolls is much lower. Among my network, for equivalent levels of public success, women get nastygrams significantly more often than men. I suspect that many men underestimate how often this happens and what the experience is like. Most women develop a thick skin about this, because what else can you do? But I wonder about the cumulative cost of containing this hostility over time. Like, what would it be like if you could just do the work without worrying about anonymous weirdos? This week, consider sending a woman whose work you admire some fan mail instead. ❤️
posts.hook: Something that surprises many of my male colleagues: Most women who achieve professional success in public get hate mail.
posts.name: Kieran Snyder

posts.text: End-of-week reflection: It's been just over 6 months since I returned to BigCo work. Here are three things about the change that have surprised me. 1) Muscle memory is deep. I was a strong enterprise operator before starting Textio and diving into startups for over a decade. Over the last few months, I have felt myself using skills that I forgot I had. Most surprisingly, my inside-enterprise operating skills have only improved thanks to my experience as a CEO. 2) Enterprise is operating with more urgency than in the past. My prior experience of enterprise, both as an employee and as a startup vendor who sold to them, was being frustrated by the slow pace. This time around, I am seeing some parts of enterprise outpace startups wrt execution. 3) Despite the above, tech companies are all kinda the same. Obviously cultures vary. But I've been surprised how similar the conversations are in big places and in small ones, especially when it comes to understanding the core levers of the business in the current market. Much of what I've experienced is what I expected, but the three things above have been real surprises. Naturally, startups and enterprises still operate differently. But breakout startups are growing faster than I've ever seen, and in many ways, enterprises are transforming operations to meet them from the other side. ** I'm Kieran Snyder, an AI exec, former CEO, and data storyteller who publishes new data and comics about AI, management, and work at nerd processor. Give me a follow if you like this stuff!
posts.hook: End-of-week reflection: It's been just over 6 months since I returned to BigCo work.
posts.name: Kieran Snyder
